skill.name: project-scaffolder
skill.description: Scaffold starter projects for common web, API, CLI, mobile, desktop, library, full stack, and monorepo stacks.
skill.summary: Create structured project starters with configuration, dependencies, tests, CI basics, and documentation guidance.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, environment, network, and credential patterns, but review shows they are mostly Markdown scaffold examples rather than hidden executable code. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found. The skill should be published with a warning because users may copy generated shell commands and placeholder secret configuration into real projects.

medium_findings.title: Executable Scaffold Commands Require Review
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many package manager, shell, build, and setup command examples. This is expected for a scaffolding skill, but copied commands can modify files, install dependencies, run hooks, or contact package registries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clearly present and would affect a user system if executed. Confidence is not higher because they are documented scaffold examples, not covert execution logic.

medium_findings.title: Placeholder Secrets and Environment Configuration
medium_findings.description: The templates include `.env` handling, database URLs, JWT secrets, and API key placeholders. These are common scaffold elements, but weak placeholder values can become real risk if reused in deployed projects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit environment, database, secret, and API key examples. The risk depends on user behavior because the values are placeholders in documentation.

low_findings.title: Local Development Network Defaults
low_findings.description: Hardcoded network values are local development examples such as localhost URLs, a FastAPI bind address, and CORS origins. No evidence found of external data exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs and bind address are visible local development settings. There is no suspicious remote domain or instruction to transmit credentials.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Misclassified Example Syntax
low_findings.description: Several high-severity patterns are false positives from Markdown examples, including Go import blocks, Cobra command names, Makefile substitution, and backtick-formatted text. No dynamic JavaScript import or Windows cmd.exe execution was found at the cited lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines are Markdown code blocks for Go and Makefile examples. The apparent dangerous tokens are normal syntax in those examples, not hidden execution by the skill.

low_findings.title: Hidden Configuration File Examples
low_findings.description: The skill references hidden files such as `.env`, `.gitignore`, and a user config file path. These are normal scaffold artifacts, but generated projects should avoid committing real secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden file references are visible documentation examples for standard project configuration. They do not instruct the agent to read or exfiltrate existing user secret files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Flags Are Contextual or False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography blocker detections map to placeholder authentication settings or ordinary words in examples. No evidence found of real credential material, custom crypto code, or malicious use of weak algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected locations are scaffold text or placeholders, not executable cryptographic implementation. Some authentication placeholders still warrant user review, so the confidence is below very high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell and Package Manager Command Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches commands for creating directories, installing dependencies, running builds, and configuring tooling. These commands are useful but should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and numerous across the template. They are not malicious by themselves, but they are operationally sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment and Secret Placeholder Templates
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes template values for environment files, database URLs, JWT secrets, and API keys. These placeholders must be replaced and protected in real projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The secret and environment examples are directly visible in the scaffold text. The risk is contextual because they are examples rather than real embedded credentials.

content.value_statement: Starting a project often requires many setup decisions before useful work begins. This skill generates practical starter structures, configuration files, tool setup, and documentation guidance for common stacks.
actual_capabilities: Provides scaffold plans for frontend, backend, mobile, desktop, CLI, library, full stack, and monorepo projects.
Includes example directory structures for React, Express, FastAPI, Go CLI, and related project types.
Suggests package manager commands for installing common runtime and development dependencies.
Drafts configuration examples for linting, formatting, testing, environment variables, GitHub Actions, and Git ignore rules.
Provides setup checklists, README guidance, and development workflow recommendations.
limitations: Generated commands and configuration examples must be reviewed before execution in a real repository.
Dependency versions and framework conventions may require updates for current releases.
Secret values, database URLs, and environment files are placeholders and must not be used in production.
It does not replace framework-specific security hardening or deployment review.

use_cases.title: Create a New Application Starter
use_cases.description: Generate a clear directory structure, toolchain, and setup checklist for a new web or API project.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Project Templates
use_cases.description: Define repeatable starter layouts with testing, formatting, linting, CI, and documentation conventions.

use_cases.title: Plan a CLI or Library Package
use_cases.description: Draft package structure, build commands, testing setup, and release-oriented files for reusable tooling.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Web App Scaffold
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a common frontend project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use project-scaffolder to create a React and TypeScript starter. Include folders, dependencies, scripts, tests, and README sections.

prompt_templates.title: API Starter With Quality Tools
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for backend project setup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use project-scaffolder to design a Node.js Express API starter with TypeScript, linting, formatting, tests, environment examples, and CI.

prompt_templates.title: Team Template Standard
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for repeatable internal scaffolding.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use project-scaffolder to create a team-ready project template for a FastAPI service. Include conventions for config, testing, documentation, and security placeholders.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Package Repository Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a complex repository structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use project-scaffolder to plan a monorepo with shared packages, app folders, CI workflow, package scripts, documentation, and onboarding notes.

output_examples.input: Create a React TypeScript starter for a small product team.
output: A proposed folder structure with components, hooks, services, styles, tests, and public assets.
A setup sequence for Vite, TypeScript, linting, formatting, test tooling, and pre-commit checks.
README sections covering installation, development, testing, build, and contribution workflow.

output_examples.input: Set up an Express API template with TypeScript.
output: A backend layout with routes, controllers, middleware, services, types, tests, and configuration files.
Dependency guidance for Express, CORS, Helmet, dotenv, TypeScript, Jest, and Supertest.
Notes for environment configuration and local development scripts.

output_examples.input: Plan a Go CLI starter project.
output: A CLI-oriented folder layout with commands, internal packages, public packages, tests, and build files.
Recommended Cobra and Viper setup plus common build, test, clean, install, and lint tasks.

best_practices: Review every generated command before running it, especially install, build, hook, and cleanup commands.
Replace placeholder secrets and database URLs before using generated configuration outside local development.
Update dependency versions and framework commands against current official documentation before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not paste generated shell commands into a privileged terminal without reading them first.
Do not commit generated `.env` files or real credentials to version control.
Do not treat scaffolded CORS, authentication, or database examples as production security defaults.

faq.question: What types of projects can this skill scaffold?
faq.answer: It covers common frontend, backend, mobile, desktop, CLI, library, full stack, and monorepo starter patterns.

faq.question: Does it run the setup commands automatically?
faq.answer: The skill provides command examples and scaffold guidance. Users should review commands before allowing any execution.

faq.question: Can it create production-ready security settings?
faq.answer: It can suggest baseline structure, but production security settings require project-specific review and hardening.

faq.question: Are the dependency versions guaranteed to be current?
faq.answer: No. The examples may need updates because package versions and framework recommendations change over time.

faq.question: Does it support monorepos?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes monorepo planning for tools such as Turborepo, Nx, and Lerna.

faq.question: Is it suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
